topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper was an insult. I stared at the B-minus, a mark that felt like a deliberate slight against my entire effort. My jaw tightened, and I slowly folded the graded paper, the sound of the crisp edges grating in the sudden silence of my room. It was a waste of time, frankly. I picked up my pencil again, not to revise, but to sketch a sharp, ugly line across the margin of the assignment, just to feel something tangible that wasn't this hollow disappointment. Why bother trying to improve something that clearly wasn't meant to be good? I tossed the paper onto the pile of discarded notes, a small, dismissive gesture, and turned my attention to something far more engaging: the intricate mechanics of a broken wristwatch I’d found in the drawer.

[story 2]

"A C-minus? Really?" I muttered under my breath, the sound thin and sharp, barely audible over the hum of the refrigerator. I slammed my textbook shut, the impact echoing in the quiet apartment. My knuckles were white where I gripped the cover. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between the desk and the window, my breath coming in short, shallow bursts. The grade felt like a poorly constructed barrier, something flimsy that had managed to trip me up. I ran a hand through my hair, messing it up deliberately, a physical manifestation of my irritation. This entire course, the late nights, the sheer mental energy I’d poured in—all reduced to this lukewarm assessment. It was insulting how easily things could fall apart.

[story 3]

I didn't even bother looking at the numerical score immediately. I just held the sheet of paper between two fingers, feeling the cheap paper yield slightly under the pressure. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped me, laced with a dry, humorless sound. I let my gaze drift over the rubric, cataloging the errors with an almost bored precision. It was amateurish, frankly. I crumpled the paper slowly, deliberately, until it was a tight, dense ball in my fist. The frustration wasn't a sudden explosion; it was a slow, seeping coldness that settled deep in my chest. I dropped the wad onto the floor, the sound muffled, and walked toward the door, the decision already made: this endeavor was beneath my notice.
